search_doc:
The alcohol ban becomes a Heineken 0.0 billboard.
Mechanisms: regulation-as-media, shelf-as-billboard, rights-circumvention, purpose-driven-product, single-sku-storefront
Idea: Heineken created special POS shelves covers, designed to sell Heineken 0.0 and help stores respect the ban. The billboard covered the entire beer shelf, leaving a hole so clients can grab a Heineken 0.0.
Insight: Argentina's alcohol-ban POS covers are a forced sales blackout — but a 0.0 beer is legally exempt, so the cover itself can be turned into a single-SKU shoppable billboard.

inferred.insight: Argentina's alcohol-ban POS covers are a forced sales blackout — but a 0.0 beer is legally exempt, so the cover itself can be turned into a single-SKU shoppable billboard.
inferred.one_liner: The alcohol ban becomes a Heineken 0.0 billboard.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted Argentina's election-day alcohol-sales ban into a one-day exclusive storefront for Heineken 0.0, posting the brand's #1 sales day since launch and a 97% beer-category share for that day.

visual.style_description: Hero photograph of an Argentine supermarket beer aisle covered by a custom blue billboard with a die-cut hole that frames a Heineken 0.0 bottle and the line 'Even if today you can't, now you can.'; competitor logo strip across the top, before/after store shots and press quotes along the bottom, three big white KPI numbers stacked on the right.

idea.rationale: Converting Argentina's mandatory election-day alcohol-aisle cover into a single-SKU shoppable billboard for Heineken 0.0 — the one beer legally exempt from the ban — is a sharp regulation-as-media flip. The die-cut window framing only the 0.0 bottle turns a forced sales blackout into the brand's exclusive storefront for 24 hours.

strategy.rationale: Heineken 0.0 is the alcohol-free extension of the global Heineken lager whose brand mission positions non-alcoholic beer as a 'now you can' product for moments when alcohol is unavailable or inappropriate, so the election-ban context is direct mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The legal context is cited specifically — National Electoral Code Article 71 prohibits alcohol sales for 24 hours during presidential elections — which is a verifiable, named regulation. No deeper consumer-behaviour insight is cited beyond the legal hook itself.

execution.rationale: Custom shelf-cover billboards with the cut-out window rolled out across supermarkets and convenience stores nationally, with on-cover messaging ('Even if today you can't, now you can.', 'Drink and vote, now you can.') and earned-press hooks in Infobae and La Nacion. The timing is tight to a single 24-hour regulatory window, which is the right amplification frame for a one-day media stunt; the rollout scope (number of stores) is not quantified on the board, which limits the orchestration read.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome — '#1 highest sales day since the launch of Heineken 0.0' — is the right business KPI for a launch SKU but is given as a rank rather than an absolute volume or revenue figure, so the scale is undisclosed. '97% of beer consumed on Election Day was Heineken 0.0' is plausible because competitors were legally banned that day, making this a near-tautology rather than a competitive win. The 43.5M media-impressions number is entrant-reported and unanchored against Argentina's media universe.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the regulation-as-media flip is elegantly on-brand for a 0.0 SKU, but the headline 97% share is a tautology of the alcohol ban and the absolute sales figures behind the '#1 day' rank are not disclosed.

one_liner: Buy a Barbie toy car, get a real used car.

Buy a Barbie toy car, get a real used car.
Mechanisms: cultural-moment-to-sale, challenger-marketplace-hack, qr-bridge-to-purchase, co-branded-trojan-horse
Idea: As a new challenger in the US used-car market, CarBravo makes an ad that turned prices for the used Barbie cars, even toy ones, with a modest budget and the green light from Mattel. We bought a full fleet of overpriced used Barbie toy cars and listed them on our commerce site designed to mirror the real CarBravo.com. Each toy car has full-sized spec sheets, filter and buy. Used toy cars at first and lowest prices. Sold like real cars sold using small budget. With each delivery, custom QR codes in our packaging connected every used toy Barbie car to listings of the same or similar real-life vehicles on CarBravo.com.
Insight: Barbie's cultural surge inflated toy-car prices to absurd levels — a perfect, low-cost Trojan horse for a challenger auto brand to ride pink hype into real used-car sales.

extracted.context: Barbie was having a moment. She was everywhere, but pink popularity came with a price: used Barbie Dream Cars (toy cars) selling at resale sites for surprising inflated prices (10x times normal value).

We bought a full fleet of overpriced used Barbie toy cars and listed them on our commerce site designed to mirror the real CarBravo.com. Each toy car has full-sized spec sheets, filter and buy. Used toy cars at first and lowest prices. Sold like real cars sold using small budget. With each delivery, custom QR codes in our packaging connected every used toy Barbie car to listings of the same or similar real-life vehicles on CarBravo.com.

metrics: 1 Toy Car Listing → 12 Real Car Views
$2.3M real used cars sold to Barbie email recipients

inferred.insight: Barbie's cultural surge inflated toy-car prices to absurd levels — a perfect, low-cost Trojan horse for a challenger auto brand to ride pink hype into real used-car sales.
inferred.one_liner: Buy a Barbie toy car, get a real used car.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked the Barbie cultural moment to drive challenger-brand used-car sales: $2.3M in real vehicles sold to Barbie toy-car buyers and CarBravo's best sales month ever, on a modest budget.

visual.style_description: Bright pink hero board with a giant pink toy convertible photographed at angle in front of a wall of phone screens showing the Barbie-themed car listings; left column carries dense body copy in white type, four pink metric badges sit at the bottom and a QR-code testimonial card anchors the lower right.

idea.rationale: Listing overpriced Barbie toy cars on a mirror of CarBravo.com — with full spec sheets, filters and QR codes that bridge to real used vehicles — is a sharp Trojan-horse mechanic that converts pink hype into actual auto retail traffic. The mirror-site detail (listings look exactly like real used-car inventory) is the bit that lifts it from gag to commerce.

strategy.rationale: CarBravo is General Motors' US used-car marketplace launched as a challenger to Carvana and CarMax, with no built-in cultural recognition, so leveraging a pop-culture moment to buy cheap awareness is a sensible challenger play. The insight — inflated Barbie toy-car resale prices during the film moment — is observable on resale sites and the Mattel green light is documented on the board, both giving the context veracity weight. The brand-mission link is thinner than the cultural hook: CarBravo sells used cars, Barbie sells nostalgia, and the connection rests almost entirely on the word 'car' rather than any shared brand purpose.

execution.rationale: A mirror commerce site stood up the Barbie inventory with realistic CarBravo.com UX, packaging carried QR codes that handed buyers off to real-vehicle listings, and the toy fleet was hand-delivered to create one-to-one CRM follow-up. The orchestration is end-to-end on a modest budget, but the activation is largely owned-channel and short-tail with no significant earned-press list reported on the board to amplify the stunt.

impact.rationale: $2.3M in real used cars sold to Barbie email recipients is the right business outcome for a used-car marketplace and is a credible, dollar-anchored result — though the cohort size ('Barbie email recipients of how many?') isn't disclosed. 'Best Sales Month Ever' is a self-claim with no baseline figure attached, and 'one toy car listing → 12 real car views' is a directional engagement proxy rather than a hard conversion metric. The 'inventory sold out' claim is largely meaningless without the fleet's absolute size.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because a low-budget cultural-hijack stunt converts pink hype into $2.3M of real used-car sales, even if most of the surrounding metrics are unbased and the brand-mission link is shallow.

Mechanisms: competitor-bill-as-currency, complementary-meal-hijack, cultural-stereotype-flip, promo-as-commentary
Idea: McDonald's wanted to show sympathy for the starving souls of Dubai, by offering them a satisfying complimentary free meal — all in exchange for their big bill.

extracted.tagline: What to eat when you've already eaten.

App / mobile flow: 'Have a fine dining bill? Join us for a free meal!' with bill upload

inferred.context: Dubai's high concentration of fine-dining restaurants leaves diners with big bills but small portions, creating an unmet post-meal hunger.
inferred.one_liner: Swap your fine-dining bill for a free Big Mac.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Trades fine-dining receipts for free McDonald's meals to feed still-hungry luxury diners, lifting footfall 43% and related sales 35% with 62M campaign reach across global trade press.

Left and right press-quote callouts flank the hero; below sits a phone mockup of the redemption flow, four metric blocks and a press-logo bar.

idea.rationale: Trading a fine-dining receipt for a free McDonald's meal — 'what to eat when you've already eaten' — is a competitor-bill-as-currency mechanic that uses rivals' invoices as the brand's own coupon. The reframe is funny and on-tone for McDonald's value positioning, though competitor-bill-redemption promos are a recognised category playbook rather than wholly original.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's is the global QSR leader whose brand mission centres on accessible value and satisfaction, so positioning the chain as the cure for fine-dining still-hunger is on-brand mission-fit. The Dubai-specific context — claimed as 'the most fine dining restaurants per capita in the world' — is cited without a named source and the implied universal frustration of 'bad fine diners…dissatisfied after paying high prices' is a stereotype rather than a researched insight. The Dubai cultural scope is precise and well-chosen for the joke.

execution.rationale: An app flow lets diners upload a fine-dining bill in exchange for a free meal, with geo-promotion concentrated around Dubai's high-end dining districts to target the moment of post-meal dissatisfaction. The case film and PR rollout landed coverage across global trade press (AdAge, Adweek, Campaign, Forbes, Contagious), but the activation is essentially one redemption mechanic rather than a multi-phase orchestration. Channel-target fit is strong for the geo-targeting choice.

impact.rationale: Headline commerce KPIs — 43% footfall increase and 35% related sales increase — are the right metrics for a QSR promo but both are percentages without disclosed bases, leaving '43% of what daily / monthly footfall?' open. The 62M campaign reach is entrant-reported and unanchored, and the 15% brand-love lift is a survey metric whose methodology is not described. The press list is overwhelmingly trade rather than consumer, suggesting the cultural conversation was concentrated in industry rather than general public.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the competitor-bill mechanic is a tidy on-brand commerce joke for Dubai, but every headline number — 43% footfall, 35% sales, 62M reach — is a percentage or claim without an anchored base.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Repurposed iFood's captive in-app audience as a free national broadcaster of the FIFA Women's World Cup, delivering a +23% order bump and 4.5M hours of streamed media when no open TV would carry the games.

idea.rationale: Turning a food delivery app into a national broadcaster for a sporting event no open TV would carry is a category-redefining inversion of what 'commerce media' is — the app's idle browse-time becomes the rights workaround. The reframe is instantly legible: every order session doubles as a watchparty.

strategy.rationale: iFood is Brazil's dominant food-delivery platform whose growth depends on time-in-app and order frequency, so converting its captive audience into a broadcast destination is direct platform mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that users spend 38 minutes per week browsing iFood is a specific behavioural data point the board grounds in, and the 2022 World Cup reach figure of 93% of Brazilians is a credible cultural anchor. The framing of FIFA as a 'self-fulfilling problem' because no Brazilian open TV broadcast all the women's games is plausibly accurate but not independently sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: All 64 Women's World Cup games were embedded live inside the delivery app via a partnership with the world's largest football streaming channels, with an in-app viewer carrying commentary and chat. The orchestration closes the loop between the broadcast and the brand's actual revenue moment — watch and order in the same screen — so the channel choice is exactly aligned with both objective and target.

impact.rationale: The +23% order lift versus the previous month is the right business outcome for a delivery platform and is the strongest impact proof on the board, though the absolute order base isn't disclosed so the magnitude can't be sized. The 4.5M streamed hours and +28 minutes in-app are credible engagement proxies for the broadcast mechanic itself. The '+3.5Bi views' and '21MM %' figures appear without context or base and read as soft claims, and the press credit is limited to ESPN — earned coverage is thinner than the scale of the activation suggests.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because it rebuilds the delivery app itself into the broadcaster of a tournament no Brazilian network would carry — and converts that watch-time directly into a +23% order bump.

search_doc:
Black Friday becomes Black-Owned Friday.
Mechanisms: name-as-content, shoppable-music-video, platform-ecosystem-activation, celebrity-as-host, purpose-driven-commerce
Idea: Transcend the singular shopping moment of Black Friday with a social-first campaign that catches fire in culture and inspires the world to search and shop Black-owned all year round.
Insight: Black Friday is the year's single biggest shopping spike but generates only 5% of holiday revenue for Black-owned businesses — turning the day's name itself into a campaign vehicle can redirect that traffic year-round.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE COMMERCE/BRONZE/Black-Owned Friday - Google (Google, Mountain View _ Google Brand Studio, San Francisco).jpg

extracted.context: Google's Black-Owned Friday campaign originated in 2020 to uplift Black-owned businesses disproportionately affected by the pandemic. This initiative remains relevant in its 4th year; only 5% of holiday shopping revenue is generated at Black-owned businesses, and 13% of Americans say they regularly shop at them. We aim to increase awareness and visibility of Black-owned businesses, nationwide and year-round, by equipping them with the power of Google's product ecosystem, including Maps, Search, Ads space, and AI tools that can supercharge their marketing.
extracted.idea: Transcend the singular shopping moment of Black Friday with a social-first campaign that catches fire in culture and inspires the world to search and shop Black-owned all year round.
execution: Music video '100% Black-Owned' fronted by Keke Palmer, a reinvention of Crystal Waters' '100% Pure Love'
Shoppable music video featuring 75 products and Black business owners nationwide
Black-owned business badge available on Google Search and Maps
Bespoke social content for business owners on Google's channels
Google.com homepage shout-out and streaming single releases on Google's social channels
Dynamic OOH rotation in NYC and LA spotlighting Black-owned businesses near the billboards
Broadcast flight during Black Friday shopping weekend
metrics: 2X social engagement compared to the previous year (355K in 2023 vs 161K in 2022)
40+ positive press mentions in major outlets

inferred.insight: Black Friday is the year's single biggest shopping spike but generates only 5% of holiday revenue for Black-owned businesses — turning the day's name itself into a campaign vehicle can redirect that traffic year-round.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-anchored Google's product ecosystem — Search, Maps, Ads, shoppable music video — around Black-owned commerce for the fourth year running, delivering 374M+ impressions, 99% positive sentiment and a 2x year-on-year social engagement lift.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of Keke Palmer at a vintage gold microphone in a glossy black tuxedo on a dark teal background, framed by the Google logotype and the Black-Owned Friday wordmark with a tomato-red 'O'; left and right columns carry strategy copy, a shoppable-grid module, OOH and tweet mockups, plus a thumbnail strip of featured business owners along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Renaming Black Friday as 'Black-Owned Friday' and routing Google's product ecosystem (Search, Maps, Ads, business badges) toward Black-owned retailers is a clear platform-purpose idea, though in its fourth year the renaming mechanic is no longer fresh. The shoppable music-video format is the one new layer this edition leans on for ideation.

strategy.rationale: Google is Alphabet's search and platform business whose commercial relevance to small merchants flows through Search, Maps, Ads and Shopping, so a campaign that mobilises those exact surfaces toward an under-represented merchant segment is direct platform mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The board grounds the case in two specific data points — 5% of holiday shopping revenue at Black-owned businesses, 13% of Americans shopping there regularly — which are plausible US retail-equity figures but cited without source on the board. The four-year continuity of the program adds credibility that this is a sustained commitment, not a one-off purpose play.

execution.rationale: The orchestration stacks a celebrity-led shoppable music video (Keke Palmer reworking 'Pure Love') against Google product surfaces (Search, Maps badges, homepage shout-out), dynamic OOH in NYC and LA that geo-rotates nearby Black-owned businesses, and broadcast media bought across the Black Friday weekend. The channels are well-matched to a discovery-and-search objective — billboards point to nearby merchants, the badge lives in Maps, the music video carries 75 products to shop — though the layers add up to a campaign in scale, not necessarily distinctiveness.

impact.rationale: 374M+ impressions and 14.6M video views are entrant-reported reach claims, not commerce outcomes, and there is no sales-lift, traffic-to-merchants or transaction-volume figure on the board — the actual business outcome a commerce campaign should demonstrate is conspicuously absent. The 2x year-on-year social engagement (355K vs 161K) is honestly base-anchored, which is good RIGOUR practice, but the absolute numbers are small relative to the impressions claim. '99% positive campaign sentiment' is a soft sentiment metric without sample size or methodology disclosed.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because it routes Google's whole product ecosystem toward Black-owned retailers at credible platform scale, but the commerce KPI — actual sales or merchant-traffic lift — is missing from the case, leaving the impact resting on reach and sentiment.

A free car until the job is secure.
Mechanisms: product-as-social-service, purpose-driven-product, friction-removal-commerce, public-private-distribution
Idea: Cars to Work: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE COMMERCE/GRAND PRIX/Cars To Work - Renault (Publicis Conseil Paris).jpg

extracted.tagline: Voitures de future fonction / No car, no job, no loan, no lease.
extracted.context: A vicious circle for society's most fragile: a creator of social exclusion. 40% of French people live in areas with no public transportation, places where a car is the only solution. 54% of French job-seekers have already turned down a position because they have no mobility solution. But often buying a car is not an option. In France, a new job starts with a three-month trial period where you can be fired any time, so you are not eligible for a car loan.
extracted.idea: Cars to Work: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on FranceTravail.fr, the French public employment service.
execution: Free Renault car loaned during the job trial period
Affordable payment only starts once the job is secured
Distributed via 50 Renault dealerships in mobility deserts
Listed on FranceTravail.fr (French public employment service)

inferred.insight: Without a car you cannot get the job, but without the job you cannot get the car — mobility is the missing rung on the employment ladder.
inferred.one_liner: A free car until the job is secure.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositions Renault from car-seller to mobility enabler by lending vehicles to 6,000 job-trialists in transit-poor regions; 8.8 million French citizens reached and broad mainstream media pickup.

visual.style_description: Cinematic horizontal photograph of a white Renault car on an empty rural road under a wide sky; the slogan 'no car, no job, no loan, no lease' loops circularly behind the car to evoke the vicious circle. Lower half is a clean editorial layout with context/idea columns, three large stat blocks, an outline map of France with dealership dots, an execution photo strip, and a customer testimonial.

idea.rationale: Inverting the car-sale flow so the vehicle is loaned free during the legal job-trial window and only billed once employment is secured is a category-redefining commerce reframe — the product becomes a payment-deferred social tool rather than a transaction. The 'no car, no job, no loan, no lease' vicious-circle slogan crystallises the mechanism in a single line, with high memorability for a finance / automotive idea.

strategy.rationale: Renault Group is France's largest legacy automaker whose stated 'human-first' positioning includes mobility access as a public good, so building a financing product around employment trial periods is direct mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight is anchored in two named, plausible French data points — 40% of French people live in transit-poor areas and 54% of job-seekers have already declined a role for lack of mobility — and the three-month-trial credit-ineligibility rule is a verifiable legal-financial fact. The board does not cite the source for the 40% / 54% figures, which is the only soft spot.

execution.rationale: The mechanism is operationalised through 50 dealerships physically located in identified mobility deserts and a placement on FranceTravail.fr, the French public employment service, which routes the product to the exact at-risk audience at the moment of job search. 6,000 vehicles made available turns a campaign idea into a working national fleet, and the dealership-plus-public-platform pairing closes the loop from job offer to mobility to repayment in one flow.

impact.rationale: The business outcome a financing-led automotive programme cares about — vehicles deployed and reach into the target population — is documented: 6,000 cars available across 50 dealerships and 8.8 million French citizens reached. The 8.8M reach figure is unanchored as a percentage of France (~13%), and the board does not yet show repayment / conversion-to-paid-lease rates, which would be the ultimate commerce KPI; press pickup spans 10 named mainstream French outlets (Le Monde, BFMTV, M6, Canal+, Arte, France 5) as a credible breadth signal even if entrant-reported.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because Renault turns its own financing product into a public mobility-for-employment programme — the car itself becomes the commerce mechanism, not an ad for one.

idea.rationale: Turning the brand's own handshake logo into the universal trigger for a real-time voucher drop is a clean broadcast-to-commerce mechanic that converts logo impressions into shoppable moments. The hunt framing gives a passive TV viewer an active reason to lean in every time the logo lands on screen.

strategy.rationale: Mercado Libre is Latin America's largest e-commerce platform, owner-operating both the marketplace and its payments arm — its commercial mission is to win Black Friday traffic against Amazon-style competitors, so a logo-triggered voucher mechanic on the region's biggest TV network is on-brand for a marketplace fighting for share of intent. The insight that integrating deals into Globo programming beats ad-break competition is plausible but presented as assertion, with no contestability data on attention-during-breaks cited. The 'world's second-largest TV network' framing for Globo is an entrant assertion rather than an independently sourced ranking.

execution.rationale: Globo programming carried in-content logo placements that triggered real-time voucher drops, with the Mercado Libre app as the redemption point — a tight two-channel orchestration that converts broadcast attention directly into checkout intent. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the sign-as-product genuinely converts the black-market demand into an upsell funnel, but both headline conversion numbers are percentages without disclosed bases and no absolute revenue is shown.

one_liner: The bottle's brew time is the sunset reward.

The bottle's brew time is the sunset reward.
Mechanisms: media-as-product, timestamp-as-trigger, b2b-gamification, data-as-content
Idea: Every bottle of Corona beer worldwide features a timestamp indicating its brewing time, with some brewed specifically at sunset — a moment we aim to claim as our own. This distinctive feature prompted our sales force to emphasise enjoying Corona at sunset. We centred our campaign around these bottle timestamps, reminding Colombian shopkeepers of the ideal time to promote our beer. By leveraging this existing asset, we encouraged shopkeepers to seek out bottles stamped with sunset times matching their city's sunset, then offering rewards to those who matched the most. With this approach, we effectively utilised our bottles as a medium to endorse the ideal drinking time, bypassing the need for additional in-store materials. This initiative successfully engaged shopkeepers, introducing them to the Corona experience for the very first time and executed the biggest loyalty campaign on a global standpoint.
Insight: For the first time, we tailored our campaign towards the nation's 550,000+ shopkeepers, providing them with a taste of the Corona experience that we're long promoted to consumers.

one_liner: The TV becomes art, the VAT drops in half.

Mechanisms: tax-loophole-hack, media-as-checkout, user-generated-pricing, platform-jailbreak
Idea: An idea: to automatically lower the price of The Frame, by letting people upload their own artwork to the TV so they get a tax discount, paying only 10% VAT and not 21% on the new The Frame TVs. And just like that, the Spanish tax system got hacked with a bit of their talent.
Insight: The Frame is the art TV from Samsung and has more than 1600 works of art from the world's leading museums and galleries. Like any other TV in Spain, when you buy it you pay 21% VAT; while far art you only pay 10% VAT.

idea.rationale: Letting buyers upload their own artwork to The Frame at point of purchase so the TV legally reclassifies as 'art' and pays the 10% art-VAT rate instead of 21% standard VAT is a tax-loophole hack that makes the product feature (art-mode display) the literal pricing mechanism. The idea is unusually category-native for Creative Commerce — the buy flow is the creative.

strategy.rationale: Samsung is the Korean electronics giant whose The Frame TV is positioned as the 'art TV', explicitly marketed as a piece of wall art that displays paintings from 1,600+ museum and gallery works when idle, so a campaign that treats the product literally as art for tax purposes is exact product-positioning fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight is grounded in a verifiable VAT-rate gap in Spanish tax law (21% standard, 10% on art) which the board states accurately and which is the load-bearing argument. The mission-fit is product-specific to The Frame rather than Samsung-corporate, which is appropriate scope.

execution.rationale: The landing page theartofhack.es hosts a paint-your-own-artwork flow, ships the artwork to the TV via Samsung's art-mode pipeline, and applies an algorithmic price adjustment at checkout that reflects the 10% VAT rate — three steps in one funnel. The 'media-as-checkout' craft is the load-bearing channel choice, since the same screen that holds the art holds the discount. The activation is single-market Spain by design and the paid amplification layers are not detailed on the board.

impact.rationale: +55% sales increase and 'one-third of annual sales in a month' are the right business outcomes for an electronics retail campaign and are mutually reinforcing, with the second figure giving a base-anchored magnitude that '+55%' alone would lack. However the comparison window for the percentage (vs which baseline period) is not specified, and the 105-unit figure cited in the execution copy is small in absolute terms for a national TV launch. 3,000 artworks uploaded is a credible mechanic-engagement count; 2.8M views is entrant-reported reach. No press list is included on the board, which limits the earned-conversation evidence.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the buy flow itself is the creative — re-classifying The Frame as art at checkout to legally halve VAT — and delivers a credible one-third-of-annual-sales burst, though the percentage's baseline window and earned-press footprint are thin.

one_liner: Buy on credit with trust, not a credit score.
search_doc:
Buy on credit with trust, not a credit score.
Mechanisms: credit-as-commerce, informal-economy-formalisation, purpose-driven-product, trust-as-currency
Idea: WeCapital, seeking financial inclusion in Mexico, launched the e-commerce of trust. An online store where unbanked customers can request credit not for cash but for items in neighborhood stores: rice, beans, food and only with one purchase: trust.
Insight: This credit system based on trust has been the only financial support for millions of unbanked families for years. The problem is that those scores just have the capacity to land a few neighbors, so we have created a larger one that can use the same system to lend to an entire country.

idea.rationale: Rebuilding an e-commerce platform that grants credit in groceries rather than cash — using the corner-store trust ledger as a credit-scoring substitute — is a category-redefining take on what 'commerce' is for the unbanked. The mechanism is also the brand product, not an awareness wrapper around one.

strategy.rationale: WeCapital is a Mexican financial-inclusion lender whose stated mission is access to credit for the unbanked, so an e-commerce platform that issues credit to people locked out of formal banking is exact product-mission fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight is grounded in a specific verifiable context — 68 million unbanked Mexicans and the long-running 'fiado' corner-store credit ritual — both of which are well-documented features of Mexico's informal economy. The board's claim that 'tienditas' have been the de facto credit system for these households for years is culturally accurate.

execution.rationale: The e-commerce flow takes a credit request, fulfils it as groceries (rice, beans, food items) via partner neighbourhood stores, and the same transaction generates an alternative credit score — three jobs done in one checkout. The channel choice (existing tienditas as fulfilment points) routes the mechanic through the trust network it borrows from, which is appropriate for the audience the platform actually serves. The board lists case-film content and press loop but the scale and phasing of paid amplification are not detailed.

impact.rationale: 9,100 families served and 15,000 customers are concrete absolute numbers, which is unusually disciplined reporting for a financial-inclusion case, though small relative to the cited 68M unbanked population. 72,000 alternative credit scores generated is a real, durable behaviour-change asset for the lender's book. The press list is largely Mexican mid-tier press rather than financial-sector validation, and there is no sales-volume or loan-book figure to size the actual commerce flowing through the platform.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because it formalises Mexico's informal corner-store credit ritual into a working e-commerce + credit-scoring product — strong mission-fit and real households served, with the absolute commerce scale left small relative to the stated opportunity.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Mined two years of abandoned-cart data to send 735K loyalty members a personalised throwback offer, lifting the post-Black-Friday week to a 318% sales jump and a 5145% ROI — the second-best sales week of the year for Samsung Brazil.

visual.style_description: Dark navy editorial board with a giant white ThrowBack Deals logotype across the top; a phone mockup at centre shows a fake archived email previewing a 2021 robot vacuum offer, flanked by smaller email mockups for refrigerator, washing machine and Galaxy phones; three big white result figures and a Samsung loyalty director quote sit on the right.

idea.rationale: Resurrecting two years of abandoned shopping carts and offering each customer their own forgotten products at the original 2021-2022 prices turns historical first-party data into a hyper-personalised commerce hook. The idea is small in expression but precise in mechanic: every email is unique to one shopper, which converts a generic post-Black-Friday discount into a memory-driven re-conversion.

strategy.rationale: Samsung is the global Korean consumer-electronics leader whose Brazilian arm runs samsung.com as a direct-to-consumer storefront, and a CRM-driven retention play on its own loyalty database is direct mission-fit for the e-commerce arm. The context is anchored in two named facts — the week after Black Friday 2022 was the worst sales week of the year for samsung.com, and 80% of online shoppers abandon carts, peaking during Black Friday — though the abandonment figure is cited without an attributed source. The insight that abandoned carts contain real demand at the wrong moment is observationally sound.

execution.rationale: The activation runs as 735,000 individually personalised emails reconstructing each member's exact 2021-2022 cart with original pricing, with one-click 'Buy Now' deep-links back to samsung.com checkout. The choice of email — explicitly framed as 'one of the most ignored mediums' — fits the recovery use case because it can carry per-recipient product data and historical context that paid media cannot. The execution is essentially a CRM craft play rather than an orchestrated multi-channel push, which is appropriate to the audience.

impact.rationale: The business outcomes are commerce-native and anchored: 318% sales lift versus the same 2022 week (the base week being explicitly identified as the worst of the year), USD 700K absolute sales attributed to the email send, and a 5145% ROI that, while a percentage, is paired with the absolute revenue figure. The board frames the week as 'the second-best sales week of the year', positioning the lift against a meaningful internal benchmark. Press pickup is thin (FOX, roastbrief, ARCHYDE) but the case does not lean on earned media.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because a tightly-targeted CRM mechanic turns Samsung's own dead-cart data into a self-funding sales channel, anchored by an absolute USD revenue number rather than a vanity percentage.

Idea: By ordering Hazy Lager at their local pub or bottle shop, Aussies could walk away with custom-made prizes from the beers they already love — like a VB massage chair, a Carlton Dry bot hut, Great Northern fishing kits, Carlton Draught fridges, snowboards, surfboards, BBQs, and many more — created, built and donated by Matilda Bay themselves. idea.rationale: Driving trial of a new lager by giving away custom merch celebrating rival lagers — a VB massage chair, Carlton Dry bot hut, Great Northern fishing kits, Carlton Draught fridges — is a competitor-love-as-promotion mechanic that openly weaponises tribal loyalty. The trick rests on a portfolio reveal: the 'rival' beers share a parent company with Matilda Bay, which makes the gag legal and converts incumbent love into umbrella brand love.

The insight that Australian lager drinkers are tribally loyal — wearing branded merch, tattooing logos, building shed shrines — is presented as an observable cultural pattern rather than a sourced study, but it is plausible and culturally precise. The wordplay is Australian-specific, not pan-Anglo.

execution.rationale: The activation runs across 100 bottle shops and pubs nationwide with on-pack / on-premise mechanics, supported by OOH, social, PR and digital. The merch programme is broad — massage chair, bot hut, fishing kits, fridges, snowboards, surfboards, BBQs — giving multiple gift tiers to sustain a promo cycle. 100 stockists is a modest physical footprint for a national Australian beer launch, capping the orchestration scale.

impact.rationale: The commerce KPIs are on-format — 160% lift in sales per store, 45% lift in pub sales, 48% lift in retail stockists — but all three are percentages without disclosed absolute baselines, leaving '160% of what per-store run-rate?' open. No press coverage is documented on the board, removing earned-media as a supporting signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the competitor-love mechanic is genuinely culturally clever for Australian beer tribes and the portfolio reveal makes it elegantly legal, but every reported lift is a percentage without an absolute base for a new-product trial campaign.
